insights.insight: The protocol settles verified purchase proof at ~$0.10 per receipt against ad tech's $5–$150 CPA for modeled attribution — a 50–200x cost delta driven by structural difference, not optimization. A cryptographically signed receipt eliminates the attribution problem entirely; it doesn't solve it more efficiently.

Chrome's deprecation of third-party cookies completes the collapse of the cross-site tracking stack that fingerprinting and data brokers were already failing to replace. This protocol produces identity-free, cross-ecosystem purchase signals — the specific signal type the industry has no functional replacement for.

Kroger, Walmart, Costco, Target, Dollar General, and Home Depot now dominate the dataset — not SaaS or digital brands. Physical receipt volume is outpacing email receipt capture, meaning the harder data collection problem (OCR + camera) is the one actually scaling.

Amazon sees Amazon purchases, Walmart sees Walmart purchases, and banks see transaction totals without line items — no single platform sees the full basket. At 3,827 verified receipts across physical and digital retail, this dataset is the first cross-ecosystem purchase record that isn't silo-bound.

Burst expansion into Kentucky, Maine, Wisconsin, and rural Texas means this is not a coastal tech-demo dataset. Physical retail dominance paired with non-metro geography produces a purchase signal distribution that approximates actual U.S. consumer behavior rather than urban early-adopter behavior.

Fetch and Ibotta hold real, receipt-verified purchase data but it is identity-attached, closed-loop, and not queryable by third parties — making it a loyalty product, not an attribution infrastructure. Brands using those networks own none of the underlying signal and cannot port it.
